name: Karakasa
description: The Karakasa-obake (唐傘お化け) is a type of tsukumogami, a Japanese yokai (spirit or monster) that originates from an umbrella. These mischievous spirits are often depicted with a single eye, a long, protruding tongue, and one leg wearing a geta (wooden sandal). They are formed when an umbrella reaches its 100th birthday and gains sentience, often becoming malevolent or playful depending on its treatment during its life as a simple object. Karakasa are known for their unpredictable nature, delighting in surprising and sometimes frightening humans. They are symbols of the supernatural and the potential for inanimate objects to develop a life of their own. The Karakasa's appearance is a visual representation of the Japanese belief in the inherent spirit or soul (kami) residing within all things, both living and inanimate. They are a common motif in Japanese folklore, art, and popular culture, representing the whimsical and often unsettling aspects of the spirit world. Their existence serves as a reminder to treat objects with respect and care, lest they become vengeful or mischievous spirits themselves. The Karakasa is more than just a haunted umbrella; it is a cultural symbol deeply embedded in the Japanese understanding of the supernatural and the interconnectedness of all things.
subtitle: The Umbrella Yokai with a Kick
appearance: A tattered umbrella with a single large eye in the center of the umbrella's canopy. It has a long, pink tongue that often lolls out. One leg protrudes from the base of the umbrella's handle, wearing a single geta sandal. The umbrella is often depicted as being old and worn, with rips and tears in the fabric.
habitat: Old houses, abandoned temples, and places where forgotten or discarded objects accumulate. They are often found in areas with high humidity.
behavior: Karakasa are generally mischievous rather than malevolent. They enjoy surprising humans with sudden appearances, loud noises, and minor acts of trickery. They may hop around on their single leg, stick out their tongue, or create small gusts of wind. Sometimes they are helpful, guiding lost travelers, but they can also be a nuisance, especially to those who mistreat objects.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Karakasa
keyMyths.description: Karakasa are said to be born from umbrellas that have reached their 100th birthday and have been discarded or mistreated. This neglect allows a spirit to inhabit the object, transforming it into a yokai.

sources.author: Shigeru Mizuki
sources.description: A popular manga and anime series that features various yokai, including the Karakasa.
